content.text: What it is

Feature flag technical debt is the accumulation of flags that are no longer needed but remain in the codebase: flags that have completed their rollout and are permanently enabled, flags whose expiry date has passed, flags with no evaluation traffic, and flags whose owning team no longer exists. Flag cleanup is the process of identifying, prioritizing, and systematically removing these flags along with their dead code branches.

In agentic AI systems, flag debt is particularly costly. Each stale flag adds a conditional branch to the agent's execution path, complicates reasoning about the system's behavior during incident investigation, and may carry a live evaluation cost on every request. A registry of 400 flags where 200 are stale is effectively a 200-flag system buried under 200 layers of dead code.
Why it matters

Flags are intended to be temporary. Their value comes from the ability to toggle behavior safely and to roll back quickly. Once a rollout is complete and the code is stable, the flag serves no purpose — but its presence imposes ongoing costs. These costs compound: a team that never removes flags eventually loses confidence in which code paths are active, must cover all flag state combinations in tests, and accepts a growing base of evaluation latency on every request.

Flag debt also creates incident risk. An operator under pressure who toggles what appears to be an inactive flag, not knowing it controls a subtly different behavior in a minority of requests, can cause a hard-to-diagnose production regression. Systematic cleanup eliminates this class of human error.
Benefits
Reduced cognitive load.

Engineers reading code with 50 live flags instead of 500 can understand the system's behavior much faster. Incident investigations proceed more quickly when there are fewer active conditional branches.
Lower evaluation latency.
Every flag evaluation, even cached, consumes CPU and memory. Removing 200 stale flags from a hot path reduces per-request overhead measurably at scale.
Simpler test matrices.
Tests for a system with N active flags must consider 2^N behavioral combinations in the worst case. Removing flags reduces test surface area and improves coverage confidence.
Cleaner registry.
A registry where every entry represents an actively used flag is trustworthy. A cluttered registry requires engineers to investigate each flag before they can trust its state.
Incident risk reduction.
Removing stale flags eliminates the risk of a flag being accidentally toggled during an incident, producing an unexpected side effect that was long forgotten.
Trade-offs and limitations
Cleanup is engineering work.
Removing a flag requires modifying source code, updating tests, and getting a PR reviewed. Teams under feature pressure routinely deprioritize this work unless there is an enforced SLA.
Risk of premature removal.
A flag that appears stale (no evaluations in 30 days) may be a safety circuit breaker intentionally kept available for emergency use. Cleanup bots must be reviewed by humans before PRs are merged.
Evaluation gap is not always stale.
A flag that controls a quarterly batch process may legitimately have zero evaluations for 80 days. Zero-evaluation detection needs awareness of scheduled vs. real-time flag usage.
Automated PR noise.
If a cleanup bot files hundreds of PRs after an initial rollout of the process, teams feel overwhelmed and stop reviewing them carefully. Phase the rollout of cleanup automation.
How it works

A cleanup pipeline runs on a schedule (weekly is common for most systems) and performs four steps. First, it discovers flags to analyze: it queries the flag management service's analytics API for evaluation counts per flag over the past 30 days, and cross-references with the registry to get expiry dates and owner information.

Second, it classifies flags into categories: expired (past their registry expiry date), stale (zero evaluations in 30 days), always-on (at 100% rollout for more than 30 days), and orphaned (owner team no longer exists). Each category has a different handling policy.

Third, for each flag in scope, the bot uses a code-search tool (or grep) to locate all flag evaluation call sites in the codebase. It then generates a pull request that removes the flag evaluation, replaces the conditional with the appropriate hardcoded behavior (the treatment branch for an always-on flag, typically), removes the flag's registry entry, and removes any tests that specifically exercise the flag path.

Fourth, the SLA enforcement layer tracks open cleanup PRs. If a PR has been open for two weeks without being merged, the bot escalates by commenting on the PR and notifying the owner team's manager. If the stale flag count exceeds a configurable threshold, CI blocks the creation of new flags in that service until the backlog is reduced.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Feature flag cleanup pipeline: 4 steps (Discover, Classify, File Cleanup PR, SLA Enforcement). Four stale flag categories: Expired (auto-PR immediately), No Evaluation (PR with 2-week SLA), Always-On (PR to hardcode and remove), Orphaned (reassign or remove). Bottom section shows tech debt costs of unremoved flags: cognitive load, evaluation overhead, test complexity, and incident risk.

content.text: The pipeline runs on a weekly schedule. Discovery reads from the flag service's analytics API and the git registry. Classification determines handling policy. PR filing uses the GitHub API. SLA enforcement tracks open PRs and escalates via Slack or email.
When to use it
When the stale flag count exceeds 10-15% of total flags.
Above this threshold, the registry is cluttered enough to impair discoverability and incident reasoning.
After initial adoption of gradual rollouts.
Teams that begin using feature flags for model promotions quickly accumulate flags from completed rollouts. A cleanup process is needed from the start.
Before major refactors.
A flag cleanup pass before a significant refactor prevents the refactor from having to reason about dozens of dead code paths.
As an ongoing automated process.
One-off cleanup sprints accumulate debt between runs. Automated weekly detection with enforced SLAs is more effective than periodic manual effort.
How to implement
Establish baselines.
Before automating, manually audit all flags and register expiry dates. This gives the cleanup bot accurate input from day one.
Instrument flag evaluations.
Ensure the flag management SDK logs evaluation counts to an analytics store that supports time-range queries. This is required for stale-flag detection.
Build the classification query.
A weekly job joins evaluation analytics with registry expiry dates and ownership. Output is a structured list of flags with their cleanup category.
Generate cleanup PRs automatically.
For each flagged item, the bot uses the GitHub API to open a PR with the code changes. The PR description includes the stale category, last evaluation date, and expiry date from the registry.
Set a creation block threshold.
When stale flags in a service exceed the threshold (e.g., 10), the CI flag-creation linter blocks new flag creation for that service.
Track cleanup metrics as a platform KPI.
Publish the stale flag count and flag age distribution on the platform engineering dashboard. Make it visible to engineering leadership.
Examples
Stale flag detector

"""stale_flag_detector.py

Identifies flags for cleanup based on evaluation analytics and registry metadata.
Outputs a JSON report for the PR-filing bot.
"""
from datetime import date, timedelta
from typing import Literal
import httpx
import yaml


STALE_NO_EVAL_DAYS = 30
ALWAYS_ON_DAYS = 30


def load_registry(path: str) -> list[dict]:
    with open(path) as f:
        return yaml.safe_load(f)["flags"]


def get_eval_stats(flag_keys: list[str], days: int) -> dict[str, dict]:
    """Fetch evaluation counts from the analytics API."""
    resp = httpx.post(
        "https://flags.internal/api/analytics/bulk",
        json={"keys": flag_keys, "days": days},
    )
    return resp.json()  # {flag_key: {count: int, last_eval: str, pct_always_on_days: int}}


def classify_flag(
    flag: dict,
    stats: dict,
    active_teams: set[str],
) -> Literal["expired", "stale", "always_on", "orphaned", "active"] | None:
    key = flag["key"]
    expires_at = date.fromisoformat(str(flag.get("expires_at", "9999-12-31")))
    owner = flag.get("owner_team", "")
    s = stats.get(key, {})

    if owner not in active_teams:
        return "orphaned"
    if expires_at < date.today():
        return "expired"
    if s.get("count", 0) == 0:
        return "stale"
    if s.get("pct_always_on_days", 0) >= ALWAYS_ON_DAYS:
        return "always_on"
    return "active"


def run_detection(registry_path: str, active_teams: set[str]) -> list[dict]:
    flags = load_registry(registry_path)
    stats = get_eval_stats([f["key"] for f in flags], STALE_NO_EVAL_DAYS)
    results = []
    for flag in flags:
        category = classify_flag(flag, stats, active_teams)
        if category != "active":
            results.append({"flag": flag, "category": category, "stats": stats.get(flag["key"], {})})
    return results


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import json
    active = {"agents-platform", "ml-team", "data-eng"}
    report = run_detection("flags/registry/agents.yaml", active)
    print(json.dumps(report, indent=2, default=str))
Code transformation: removing an always-on flag

# Before cleanup: flag controls which model to use
from openfeature import api

def get_model(user_id: str) -> str:
    ctx = EvaluationContext(targeting_key=user_id)
    use_new = client.get_boolean_value(
        "agents.summarizer.use_claude_3_7", False, ctx
    )
    if use_new:
        return "claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219"
    return "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"


# After cleanup: flag is at 100% and has been removed
# The treatment branch is hardcoded; the old branch is deleted.
def get_model(user_id: str) -> str:
    return "claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219"
Real-world example

A platform team at a SaaS company with 12 microservices had accumulated 480 feature flags over 18 months of active development. A manual audit found that 210 were stale: 90 expired, 70 with no evaluation traffic, 35 permanently at 100%, and 15 orphaned. The dead code introduced by these flags amounted to approximately 8,000 lines.

The team deployed a cleanup bot that filed PRs in weekly batches of 20 (to avoid overwhelming reviewers). They applied a two-week merge SLA and escalated to engineering managers for overdue PRs. After three months, stale flag count dropped from 210 to 18 and was maintained below 25 as an ongoing KPI. Engineers reported that incident investigations were noticeably faster because they no longer needed to verify whether suspect flags were active or dead code.
Common mistakes
Treating zero evaluations as always stale.
A flag controlling a quarterly batch process will have zero evaluations for months at a time. Check whether the flag is expected to be dormant before filing a cleanup PR.
Auto-merging cleanup PRs.
A human must review and approve cleanup PRs. An automated merge of a cleanup PR that incorrectly removed an active safety flag would cause an immediate incident.
Filing hundreds of PRs at once.
Batching PRs in weekly groups of 10-20 prevents reviewer fatigue and ensures each PR gets careful attention.
No enforcement mechanism.
A bot that files PRs but has no SLA enforcement mechanism produces PRs that accumulate unreviewed. Enforcement (escalation, creation blocking) is what makes cleanup stick.
Removing the flag without removing the dead code branch.
A flag removal that only deletes the registry entry and the evaluation call leaves the unused code branch, which is the main source of debt. Both the branch and the evaluation must be removed.
Best practices
Register expiry dates for every flag at creation time; no permanent flags allowed.
Run detection weekly and file PRs in batches to avoid reviewer fatigue.
Require human review for all cleanup PRs; never auto-merge flag removals.
Enforce a two-week merge SLA and escalate to engineering managers for overdue PRs.
Block new flag creation when stale flag count exceeds the service-level threshold.
Track stale flag count and flag age distribution as visible platform engineering KPIs.
Remove both the flag evaluation call sites and the dead code branch — not just the registry entry.
Performance and scalability

For systems with thousands of flags, the detection query against the analytics store should be batched (100-200 flags per API call) to avoid rate limits. The PR-filing step should be rate-limited to match the repository's API quota. For very large codebases, code-search-based call site detection can be parallelized across services. The overall pipeline runtime is dominated by the code-search step; cache the call site map between runs and only re-scan files modified since the last run.

Security and reliability

Cleanup PRs that incorrectly remove an active flag can cause production incidents. Every cleanup PR must include a clear description of the stale category (expired, no evaluation, always-on) and the last observed evaluation date. Reviewers must confirm that the flag is genuinely unused before merging. For flags classified as always-on, the PR must remove the disabled branch rather than hardcoding the disabled behavior — removing a safety check silently is a serious risk.

For operational safety flags (those used as emergency circuit breakers), consider a separate governance track that requires explicit team leadership sign-off before removal, regardless of evaluation count or expiry date.
Key takeaways
Feature flag technical debt — accumulated stale, expired, and always-on flags — imposes cognitive load, evaluation overhead, and incident risk that grows with flag count.
Automated weekly detection with four stale categories (expired, no evaluation, always-on, orphaned) covers the full population of cleanup candidates.
Filing cleanup PRs is necessary but insufficient; SLA enforcement with escalation and creation blocking is what drives actual cleanup.
Cleanup must remove both the registry entry and the dead code branch; a registry-only deletion leaves the dead code and its maintenance burden intact.
Human review is required before merging every cleanup PR; never auto-merge flag removals.
Emergency circuit-breaker flags need a separate governance track; zero evaluations does not mean they are safe to remove.
Track stale flag count and flag age distribution as visible KPIs to create organizational accountability for flag hygiene.
